facility_name: ORNL Summit Supercomputer — Oak Ridge Leadership Computing Facility
full_address: Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, TN 37830
customer_type_if_known: DOE scientific research, open science community
build_phase_if_known: Operational since 2018, successor Discovery due 2028
cooling_type: direct liquid cooling (cold plates on CPUs/GPUs) + rear-door heat exchangers
opportunity_summary: Summit consumes ~15MW with warm-water (70F) DLC cold plates on all CPUs and GPUs plus rear-door heat exchangers. Cooling drops to 10% of ops cost vs predecessors. Being decommissioned as Frontier/Discovery come online — cooling lessons inform next-gen DOE systems.
confidence_reason: OLCF official documentation, DOE press releases, and Wikipedia TOP500 entries confirm 15MW peak power and DLC architecture with warm-water cold plates.

sources.evidence_snippet: Summit's peak power consumption will be about 15 MW. Heat removal is performed using medium temperature direct liquid cooling and rear-door heat exchanger based secondary cooling loop.

sources.source_name: OLCF — Warmer Water to Cool Future Supercomputers
sources.evidence_snippet: Summit's systems have the ability to utilize moderate water temperature (70F) for cooling. Cooling is going to drop to about 10 percent of operations costs.

data_gaps: Specific cooling vendor not confirmed in sources

pipeline_detail.expansion_details: Successor Discovery (exascale) expected 2028, Lux AI cluster expected 2026
pipeline_detail.pipeline_evidence_snippet: Summit operational since June 2018. 15MW peak power with warm-water DLC.

Sophisticated liquid-cooling capabilities efficiently remove heat from processors, GPUs and switches through Cooling Distribution Units (CDUs). Warm (85F/29.4C) water cooling.

sources.evidence_snippet: Electrical circuits encased in fluid. Whole computers, power cables, dropped into non-conductive liquid. 100% heat capture, up to 70% power reduction. Submer Technologies system.
